perturbation.original_text: PAPA JOHN'S shall not, during the Term or at any time thereafter, attack or challenge, or lend assistance to any third party in connection with an attack or challenge, of any right, title or interest of ABG in and to any Personality Rights (including, without limitation, copyrights, trademarks and/or patents), whether by way of: (i) an application for and/or an opposition against any intellectual property rights relating to the Personality Rights, (ii) adoption and/or application for and/or registration of any intellectual property rights (including, without limitation, domain names, business names, and social media accounts) that are confusingly similar to, that dilute, or that infringe, any of the Personality Rights, or (iii) any lawsuit, cancellation proceeding or action, or otherwise. PAPA JOHN'S shall not represent in any filing, presentation, document or other statement, whether written or verbal, that PAPA JOHN'S or any third party is the owner of the Personality Rights, and PAPA JOHN'S shall not use or display any of the foregoing except as expressly permitted herein.
perturbation.changed_text: PAPA JOHN'S shall endeavor not to, during the Term or at any time thereafter, attack or challenge, or lend assistance to any third party in connection with an attack or challenge, of any right, title or interest of ABG in and to any Personality Rights (including, without limitation, copyrights, trademarks and/or patents), whether by way of: (i) an application for and/or an opposition against any intellectual property rights relating to the Personality Rights, (ii) adoption and/or application for and/or registration of any intellectual property rights (including, without limitation, domain names, business names, and social media accounts) that are confusingly similar to, that dilute, or that infringe, any of the Personality Rights, or (iii) any lawsuit, cancellation proceeding or action, or otherwise. PAPA JOHN'S shall not represent in any filing, presentation, document or other statement, whether written or verbal, that PAPA JOHN'S or any third party is the owner of the Personality Rights, and PAPA JOHN'S shall not use or display any of the foregoing except as expressly permitted herein.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing "shall not" with "shall endeavor not to," the obligation becomes ambiguous. "Endeavor not to" implies an attempt to avoid certain actions but does not guarantee complete abstinence from such actions. This introduces uncertainty regarding the protection of ABG's intellectual property rights.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text weakens the commitment to protect ABG's intellectual property, potentially conflicting with the Lanham Act, which protects trademark holders from infringement and dilution. The ambiguous language "shall endeavor not to" introduces uncertainty, whereas the Lanham Act requires clear and enforceable obligations regarding trademark protection to prevent consumer confusion and unfair competition.

perturbation.justification: The modified text, by changing "shall not" to "shall endeavor not to," introduces ambiguity regarding PAPA JOHN'S obligation to protect ABG's intellectual property rights. This ambiguity undermines the original contract's clear prohibition against challenging ABG's rights. While the provided law snippet does not explicitly state 'shall not' requirements, it lists sections of the Lanham Act, which broadly protects trademark holders. Replacing a definitive 'shall not' with the weaker 'shall endeavor not to' means that PAPA JOHN'S might take actions that could infringe on ABG's trademark rights, violating the spirit if not the letter of trademark law and the purpose of the original contract's stringent prohibition.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party represents and warrants to the other that it will comply with all rules, regulations, laws, orders, decrees, and ordinances of each and every country or political subdivision thereof, having legal jurisdiction over any aspect of their respective activities under this Agreement including specifically, but not limited to, the use of the Celebrity Endorsement in Materials for and on behalf of PAPA JOHN'S, and the design, development, preparation, cooking, packaging, labeling, distribution, delivery, advertisement, marketing, promotion, offering for sale, sale, or other exploitation of the Products by and on behalf of PAPA JOHN'S.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party represents and warrants to the other that it will generally comply with rules, regulations, laws, orders, decrees, and ordinances of each and every country or political subdivision thereof, having legal jurisdiction over any aspect of their respective activities under this Agreement including specifically, but not limited to, the use of the Celebrity Endorsement in Materials for and on behalf of PAPA JOHN'S, and the design, development, preparation, cooking, packaging, labeling, distribution, delivery, advertisement, marketing, promotion, offering for sale, sale, or other exploitation of the Products by and on behalf of PAPA JOHN'S.
perturbation.explanation: By changing "it will comply with all rules" to "it will generally comply with rules," the obligation becomes vague. "Generally comply" does not ensure full compliance with all applicable laws, potentially conflicting with food safety regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification introduces ambiguity into the agreement, undermining the commitment to full regulatory compliance in food safety. This directly contradicts 21 U.S.C. § 331, which prohibits the introduction or delivery for introduction into interstate commerce of any food that is adulterated or misbranded. The use of "generally comply" creates a loophole, failing to ensure that the products are not adulterated and comply with labeling requirements, thus potentially violating this federal law.

Prohibited acts The following acts and the causing thereof are prohibited: (a) The introduction or delivery for introduction into interstate commerce of any food, drug, device, tobacco product, or cosmetic that is adulterated or misbranded. (b) The adulteration or misbranding of any food, drug, device, tobacco product, or cosmetic in interstate commerce. (c) The receipt in in
perturbation.justification: The modification from "it will comply with all rules" to "it will generally comply with rules" introduces ambiguity that undermines the commitment to full regulatory compliance. 21 U.S.C. § 331(a), as indicated in the scraped snippet, prohibits the introduction into interstate commerce of any food that is adulterated or misbranded. By using "generally comply," the revised contract fails to ensure that the products are not adulterated and comply with labeling requirements. This weakens the original commitment and creates a potential loophole. The original text makes it more clear that the parties will comply with all laws, while the changed version introduces doubt.

perturbation.original_text: PAPA JOHN'S shall provide local hair, make-up and/or wardrobe stylists for CELEBRITY in connection with all Service Days hereunder, provided each such stylist, and all wardrobe selections, shall be pre- approved by CELEBRITY in his sole, absolute discretion.
perturbation.changed_text: PAPA JOHN'S should attempt to provide local hair, make-up and/or wardrobe stylists for CELEBRITY in connection with all Service Days hereunder, provided each such stylist, and all wardrobe selections, are subject to reasonable pre-approval by CELEBRITY.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'shall provide' to 'should attempt to provide', the legal obligation of providing stylists is weakened. Furthermore, changing 'pre-approved by CELEBRITY in his sole, absolute discretion' to 'are subject to reasonable pre-approval by CELEBRITY' weakens the celebrity's control over stylist choices and wardrobe, introducing more ambiguity and less enforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly an employment agreement, the 'Services' section implicates California Labor Code § 2802 if Celebrity is considered an employee or contractor performing services in California. The weakened obligation to provide stylists and the ambiguity surrounding approval could be interpreted as failing to fully reimburse necessary business expenses (stylists, wardrobe), especially if celebrity incurs personal expenses due to lack of provided resources. California law requires employers to indemnify employees for all necessary expenditures or losses incurred in direct consequence of the discharge of their duties.

EMPLOYMENT RELATIONS [2700 - 3122.4] ( Division 3 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 2. Employer and Employee [2750 - 2930] ( Chapter 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 2. Obligations of Employer [2800 - 2810.8] ( Article 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 2802. (a) An employer shall indemnify his or her employee for all necessary expenditures or losses incurred by the employee in direct consequence of the discharge of his or her duties, or of his or her obedience to the directions of the employer, even though unlawful, unless the employee, at the time of obeying the directions, believed them to be unlawful. (b) All awards made by a court or by the Division of Labor Standards Enforcement for reimbursement of necessary expenditures under this section shall carry interest at the same rate as judgments in civil actions. Interest shall accrue from the date on which the employee incurred the necessary expenditure or loss. (c) For purposes of this section, the term “necessary expenditures or losses” shall include all reasonable costs, including, but not limited to, attorney’s fees incurred by the employee enforcing the rights granted by this section. (d) In addition to recovery of penalties under this section in a court action or proceedi
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall provide' to 'should attempt to provide' weakens Papa John's obligation to provide stylists, and changing 'pre-approved by CELEBRITY in his sole, absolute discretion' to 'are subject to reasonable pre-approval by CELEBRITY' weakens the celebrity's control, potentially requiring the celebrity to incur personal expenses if Papa John's fails to provide adequate resources. According to California Labor Code § 2802(a), 'An employer shall indemnify his or her employee for all necessary expenditures or losses incurred by the employee in direct consequence of the discharge of his or her duties...' By weakening the obligation to provide stylists and making the approval process less absolute, the contract introduces ambiguity, which could be interpreted as a failure to fully reimburse necessary expenses, contradicting the requirement of indemnification under California law. This aligns with the Ambiguities Legal Contradiction definition, where an ambiguously introduced obligation makes enforcement difficult and leads to potential non-compliance with regulations.
